Rounded corners and decorative jewel/gem ornaments at top-center and corners give a friendly, charming, toy-diorama feel. No grim or dark tones anywhere. Fully consistent with Echoes of Wisdom bright mood. Technical (40%): Pass. Hard 1px outlines are visible and crisp at all zoom levels. The gold border shows clear 2-3 tone hard shading (highlight rim, midtone body, shadow edge). Pixel-grid-snapped construction is evident in the corner bracket details and gem decorations. The ornament sprites are hand-pixeled with good shape clarity. Only minor weakness: the cream text-body interior is a flat fill with no pixel-level texture or inner-shadow detail, which the Denzi peer reference suggests should have more granularity. Does not drop the score below pass threshold.

Silhouette reads clearly at 1x — dual stacked rectangles with rounded corners instantly register as dialog panel. At 4x, pixel art is crisp with clean edges. Color palette is warm amber/gold with cream, evoking friendly classic RPG (Zelda-adjacent) rather than distinctly cute/toy charm. Red gem and blue accent icons add personality and pass sticker test for the title bar. Dark bg: golden frame pops well, strong contrast. Light bg: frame still legible but softens noticeably, the cream lower text box nearly merges with a bright background. Main charm gap: the lower text area is plain beige — no decorative personality, slightly mushy against similar-value backgrounds. The dotted inner border on the lower box is a nice touch but too subtle to rescue it. Overall: clear silhouette, decent charm, but falls short of the toy/sticker-grade cuteness threshold. Passes readability, marginal on charm depth and light-bg contrast.

Panel is dominated by warm amber-gold borders, parchment-cream interiors, and golden-yellow fill — a tight analogous warm palette with high internal harmony. Outlines/shadow edges use dark warm brown (not pure black or grey), satisfying the colored-darks requirement. A small teal-blue gem icon provides a textbook warm-cool complementary accent that energizes without clashing. Red-orange decorative flourishes stay in the warm family. All hues map cleanly to the Echoes reference swatch (amber, parchment gold, teal, red-orange all present). Mood reads BRIGHT and WARM — zero goth, zero cold-corporate, zero neon. Saturation is rich but controlled. No muddy desaturated areas detected. The neutral grey is the sprite-sheet canvas background only and is not part of the panel asset.

The panel_dialog_v01 is immediately locatable against the dark dungeon background — large warm gold/amber border with cream interior creates strong contrast against cool teal-black tiles. Spottable well within 0.5s. Pixel art style (ornate corner face-icons, chunky pixelated border) is consistent with existing game UI (matches amber 攻击 button palette). Both the upper golden section and lower cream section have high tonal contrast with the dungeon floor. No confusion with game entities — clearly reads as a UI overlay panel. Echoes cleanliness is good: warm retro palette, no visual noise. Minor issue: both interior zones are empty (no text content), making it feel placeholder; this does not affect readability but would be a content concern at runtime. Arrow marker position aligns to the right edge of the panel near the 压板 button, but the panel itself is unmistakable. Overall strong integration.

The dialog panel sprite itself (panel_dialog_v01) uses warm golden-yellow and parchment/cream tones with decorative corner ornaments — this palette is coherent with cute fairy-tale aesthetics. However, the surrounding scene context is dark teal/near-black dungeon tiles with deep red accents, which reads as dungeon-crawler rather than bright sunny magical. The panel itself passes tone check (warm golds, parchment, rounded decorative frame), but it floats over a backdrop that conflicts with the target mood. Sprite-in-isolation: 8/10. Sprite-in-scene: 6/10. Averaged to 7 marginal — the panel is on-tone but the scene integration exposes a mood mismatch worth flagging.

issues: panel occupies ~38% of screen width and ~37% of screen height, dominating the game world view and reducing toy-diorama feel
dialog panel is significantly larger than the 32x32 player character visible at left edge, breaking relative scale hierarchy
two-section panel layout creates a wallpaper-like fullscreen overlay impression rather than a small in-world popup
panel decorative corner icons (~30x30px) are at the upper limit for HUD icon budget
verdicts.reasoning: The panel_dialog_v01 composite shows a large dialog panel centered on screen measuring approximately 480x260px against a 1280x690 viewport. As a UI overlay panel this is a different category than world entities, so direct player-size comparison is less critical, but the panel still occupies a substantial portion of the playfield. The top golden section with decorative scroll corners and the bottom cream text area together create a visually heavy overlay. The panel width (~38% of screen) and its vertical positioning over the center of the play area significantly obscures the game world, pushing toward a wallpaper/modal feel rather than a lightweight diorama hint. Corner ornament icons appear around 28-32px which edges past the ≤24px HUD icon guideline. The pixel-art style is consistent with the scene aesthetic and perspective is flat/top-down matching the game. Score 6: style matches but size is marginal-to-large for a diorama feel.

At 8x zoom, multiple AI tells are visible. (1) Gold/orange border transitions through soft gradient blending rather than 2-3 hard pixel tones — characteristic diffusion model output rather than deliberate pixel art shading. (2) Edges are anti-aliased with sub-pixel blending; not crisp single-pixel steps expected of authored pixel art. (3) Scattered pink/red stray pixels visible along the top and right edges of the outer frame — not part of any intentional color. (4) The face medallion icon at upper-left shows mushy/melting detail: internal forms lose pixel-grid alignment at 8x and bleed into surroundings. (5) Bottom panel inner decorative border is geometrically asymmetric — curved notch indentations appear on left/top side but the right side geometry differs, a hallmark of AI spatial confusion. (6) Outline weight varies across the same continuous border segment (1px in places, 2px in others, missing at some corners). Positive: overall semantic structure is coherent (header + body dialog layout), fill areas are relatively clean, color palette is consistent. The issues are enough to flag as marginal; the asymmetric inner border and noise pixels are the clearest hard fails.

Peer sheet is classic Denzi dungeon tileset: cool grey/teal/dark palette, heavy black pixel outlines on every element, high detail density with dithered stone textures, directional shadows, muted color temperature throughout. Candidate panel_dialog_v01 fails on multiple style axes independent of item-type: (1) Color temperature — candidate is warm gold/cream/amber while entire peer set is cool-grey/dark; this is a palette identity failure, not an item-type allowance. (2) Palette overlap — near zero; peers use charcoal, slate, teal, dark crimson; candidate uses gold (#C8A840 range), ivory, warm brown — no shared hues. (3) Outline weight/color — peers uniformly use bold black pixel outlines; candidate substitutes a decorative golden border frame with no black silhouette outline, creating a fundamentally different visual grammar. (4) Detail density — peers are texture-dense with micro-pixel work filling every cell; candidate panels are mostly flat fill with sparse ornament. (5) Shadow — peers show consistent drop/inner shadows; candidate shows minimal to no shadowing. The golden urn ornaments and corner rosettes have competent pixel craft but still in a warm heraldic register alien to the Denzi dungeon palette. Charm dimension: peers are utilitarian-grim, not cute-Echo register; candidate is decorative-elegant but still not Echo-cute — neutral/no penalty here. Overall: too many style-axis failures across color, outline, density, and shadow to pass even allowing for UI-panel item-type latitude.

Candidate (right panel): golden-bordered dialog box with parchment interior — warm, Zelda-adjacent, broadly on-tone for a Nintendo family-friendly fairy-tale target. No grimdark, no fan-service, no cold-corporate treatment in the candidate itself. However, the peer reference assets (left tileset) are a significant red flag: heavy grey stone dungeon tiles, red blood stains, dark teal portals, skull/bone debris, and fire-lava elements — a textbook desaturated dungeon-crawler palette that directly violates the bright/warm/whimsical target. This peer context signals systemic tonal drift in the broader asset library even if the dialog panel survives on its own merits. The candidate panel is also somewhat generic (plain dual-rectangle layout, standard RPG gold frame) — it passes the 'not grimdark' bar but lacks the toy-diorama warmth and distinctive fairy-tale charm (no organic curves, no playful motifs, no hand-crafted softness) needed to firmly anchor the Echoes/Hyrule target aesthetic. Score held at marginal pending asset library audit.

EMOTIONAL (60%): Bright saturated cyan/teal blade with warm orange-gold crossguard nails the Hyrule palette — saturated blues and warm accent yellows squarely in the friendly Zelda color vocabulary. The overall read is heroic-toy, not grim; silhouette is clean and approachable for a sword prop. Charming, not threatening. Score ~8/10. TECHNICAL (40%): 1px hard outlines visible cleanly at all zoom levels, no anti-aliasing bleed. Blade uses 2-3 hard tones (highlight strip, mid cyan, dark edge) — proper hard shading. Pixels are grid-snapped; the diagonal staircase is clean. Compares well to Denzi peer reference for clean silhouette and outline discipline, even though Denzi palette is more muted. Minor issue: the crossguard detail at native/1x is quite small and slightly hard to parse, but readable at 2x. Combined weighted: 0.6*8 + 0.4*8 = 8.0.

issues: silhouette too thin and elongated at 1x — nearly unreadable as a sword without context
no roundness or softness in shape — blade is sharp/edgy which conflicts with Echoes of Wisdom toy-charm aesthetic
guard/crossguard is tiny and blends into the blade at 1x
orange accent pixels on hilt are charming but insufficient to rescue overall coldness
contrast is acceptable on dark bg but the thin diagonal silhouette reads as a line/stick not a sword at 1x
verdicts.reasoning: The pixel art sword has decent contrast on both dark and light backgrounds at 4x, and the cyan/teal blade color with orange hilt accents shows some personality. However it fails the sticker test at 1x — the silhouette is too thin and diagonal to be instantly readable as a sword; it reads as a line or scratch. The shape is angular and aggressive rather than round/soft/toylike as required by the Echoes of Wisdom charm reference. There is no chunky friendliness: the blade tapers to a sharp point, the guard is minimal, and the overall impression is more generic RPG weapon than cute memorable icon. Clear-but-charmless at 4x, mushy-and-unreadable at 1x — lands in marginal territory.

Blade is a highly saturated cyan/sky-blue — direct match to 'sky blues' target. Guard accents are warm amber/orange — matches 'sunset oranges' and creates a classic warm-cool complementary harmony. Handle uses warm tan consistent with parchment-golds. Shadow pixels on the blade read as deep teal rather than neutral black/grey, satisfying the colored-darks requirement. Both hues (cyan, orange) are present in the Echoes reference palette. Mood is bright and cheerful, not grim or cold. No muddy desaturated color, no neon oversaturation, no corporate cold tones. Panel backgrounds are dark charcoal but these are display/preview artifacts, not part of the sprite itself. Overall palette compatibility and internal harmony are strong.

The sword_iron_v00 is clearly spottable: bright cyan-blue blade with warm gold handle creates strong contrast against the dark reddish-brown dungeon tile background. Locatability is good — the asset pops within 0.5s due to the saturated teal color. Silhouette reads as a sword at a 45-degree dropped-item angle, which is natural but slightly reduces the fastest archetype recognition versus a fully upright orientation. The pixel art style matches the Echoes aesthetic cleanly; no tonal clash with UI or other entities. Dark-area visibility is acceptable — the bright blade holds up on the darker tile zones. Minor issue: the underlying tile texture has moderate visual noise (red-flecked dark tiles) that introduces slight clutter around the sprite edges, but the sword does not disappear into it. Not confused with the creature/echo entity visible in the nearby box. Overall integration is solid but just short of clean pass due to the noisy tile placement and angled silhouette.

The sword_iron_v00 sprite is a small pixel-art sword with a bright cyan/teal blade and simple hilt. It is not out-of-tone (no grimdark, no horror, no aggressive styling), and the bright cyan coloring gives it a mildly magical quality that fits the fairy-tale palette. However, it reads as a generic starter-RPG weapon rather than something distinctly cute, whimsical, or fairy-tale — it lacks the round softness, sparkle detail, or warm-palette saturation that would make it strongly on-tone. The base scene itself is quite dark (dungeon-crawler dark teal grid), which the sword does not worsen, but the sprite alone does not elevate the mood toward the target aesthetic either. Marginal: acceptable for use but could benefit from softer shape language or a warmer highlight color.

The sword_iron_v00 is rendered diagonally in the scene, which is appropriate for a top-down weapon item. Its diagonal span covers roughly 1.5–2 grid tiles; projected onto axes it is approximately equal to or slightly smaller than one tile (≈player size). The 50–75% rule targets 16–24px for a 32px player, making this borderline oversized — it reads closer to 80–95% of player rather than clearly sub-player. No egregious proportion error: the sword does not overwhelm the scene and stays well below summon/creature scale. Pixel-art style and perspective are internally consistent with the top-down diorama aesthetic. The diagonal orientation correctly implies ground-placement. Minor size creep is the only flag; no wallpaper feel, no perspective mismatch.

Pixel art format inherently suppresses most AI tells, but adversarial inspection reveals: (1) The blade checkerboard highlight pattern is algorithmically regular — each teal/light-teal alternation is perfectly spaced in a way that suggests generation rather than hand-placement; (2) Crossguard asymmetry — at 8x zoom the left arm of the guard is 1-2px shorter/different than the right, inconsistent with deliberate pixel art craft; (3) Outline corner pixels show minor weight inconsistency (some corners have 1px diagonal, others have 2px L-turns) indicating non-uniform outline generation; (4) No AA edges detected — clean step edges throughout, positive sign; (5) No sub-pixel features detected — pixel boundaries are hard; (6) Solid areas (background, blade body) are free of noise pixels; (7) No color bleed between regions; (8) Semantic coherence is adequate — blade, guard, grip, and pommel are all present and recognizable. The sprite is functional pixel art but the overly regular highlight pattern and minor guard asymmetry are mild AI generation signatures. Not enough to fail outright, but not a clean pass.

Peer set (helmets, axes, arrows, spears) operates in a neutral-to-warm palette: grays, silvers, browns, golds. Candidate sword_iron_v00 presents a saturated cool blue/cyan blade with a bright highlight streak — zero overlap with the peer warm-neutral palette. (1) Outline weight: acceptable 1-2px dark outline, consistent with peers. (2) Outline color: dark near-black, consistent. (3) Shadow direction: indeterminate at this resolution; no clear conflict. (4) Shadow count: single shadow layer apparent, consistent with peers. (5) Highlight intensity: candidate highlight is vivid and saturated vs. peers' subdued sheen — notable break. (6) Color temperature: FAIL — peers are neutral/warm, candidate is distinctly cool. Largest single defect. (7) Detail density: roughly comparable pixel density, acceptable. (8) Proportion: at matching scale sword reads as appropriately sized item; no penalty for item-type size difference. (9) Rendering style: pixel art consistent with Denzi family conventions. (10) Palette overlap: FAIL — candidate blue/cyan appears nowhere in peer palette; minimal overlap. (11) Charm level: candidate retains cute pixel-art appeal, consistent with peer charm register. Summary: two hard failures (color temperature, palette overlap) plus one soft failure (highlight intensity). Iron sword should read gray-silver not bright blue; candidate currently reads as crystal or magic sword, breaking material authenticity within the family.

The candidate sword (right, 4x) renders in a cool blue-cyan palette that conflicts with the target 'warm/sunny Hyrule / toy-diorama' aesthetic. An iron sword reads expected, but the icy blue tint pushes toward generic dungeon-crawler or CRPG rather than whimsical Nintendo family-friendly warmth. The peer reference row (left) includes Viking-horned helmets with dark metallic grey tones — these lean slightly toward desaturated dungeon-crawler, providing a cooler contextual frame that further shifts the composite away from the bright/charming fairy-tale target. The pixel art scale and simplicity are consistent with a wholesome retro style, preventing a hard fail, but neither the sword's hue nor the helmet references evoke the sun-drenched, toy-diorama warmth of the target tone. No grimdark, occult, or fan-service elements detected — the drift is toward generic-RPG cold rather than any hard-fail category.

Emotional (60%): Warm orange/yellow starburst center with a light-blue magical ring reads as charming and bright — very Echoes-of-Wisdom. The star silhouette is round and friendly, not violent or grim. Saturated hues (golden yellow, vivid orange, sky blue) hit the Hyrule toy-diorama mood convincingly. Score ~8. Technical (40%): Hard 1-px dark-brown outline pixels are present and grid-snapped with no anti-aliasing artefacts. The center uses 2–3 discrete tones (bright yellow core → medium orange → dark orange/brown rim), matching the Denzi peer reference's hard-shading conventions. Scattered spark pixels are pixel-grid-aligned and asymmetric in a hand-pixeled way. Reference parity with Denzi sprite outlines and shading density is solid. Score ~8. Weighted: 0.6×8 + 0.4×8 = 8.0. Minor note: the blue ring is atypical for an explosion but functions as a magical-energy marker consistent with Zelda VFX language, so not penalised.

issues: 1x silhouette nearly unreadable — collapses to featureless blob
scattered outer particles create mushy/indistinct edge at all scales
light-blue ring particles lose contrast on light background
no strong outer silhouette shape — burst outline is irregular and soft
verdicts.reasoning: The 4x renders show genuine charm: a central star motif surrounded by a cyan particle ring and outer orange sparks gives this explosion real personality and a toy-like, friendly feel — the star is the standout detail. Color palette (gold, cyan, orange) is warm and sticker-worthy. However, charm is undermined by silhouette clarity failures. At 1x the sprite is an unreadable dot — no star, no ring, just a small smear. At 4x the scattered pixel particles around the perimeter create a fuzzy, indistinct outer edge rather than a clean readable burst silhouette; the shape reads as 'blob with stuff around it' rather than a crisp explosion icon. On the light background the blue particles nearly disappear, reducing the ring detail significantly. The result is charming-but-mushy: passes the personality test, fails the instant-readability and both-background tests. Needs either a stronger outer silhouette boundary or reduced particle scatter to push into pass territory.

The explosion VFX uses a tightly harmonious golden-orange core star against a sky-blue halo ring, with scattered amber/orange spark pixels radiating outward. This orange+sky-blue complementary pairing is bright, warm-dominant, and highly saturated — exactly aligned with the Echoes-tuned palette. The golden orange maps directly to the parchment-gold/sunset-orange swatches in the reference; the sky-blue halo matches the sky-blue swatch cleanly. No muddy desaturated tones, no cold corporate greys, no near-black shadows within the asset itself. The fourth panel on parchment background confirms the asset reads warmly on light ground as well. Dark charcoal panels 2–3 are display-context backgrounds only, not part of the asset color design. Internal harmony is excellent — the complementary orange/blue creates energetic contrast without clashing. Mood is unmistakably BRIGHT and WARM. Marginal concern: dark display-panel backgrounds are near-charcoal rather than the preferred colored-dark (deep teal/plum), but these are background panels not the VFX asset itself, so no deduction. Overall a strong, palette-compatible pass.

The explosion VFX (vfx_explosion_2_v04) is immediately spottable against the grey brick background. The orange/yellow flame ring with cyan/white inner detail provides strong color contrast against the neutral grey tiles — locatability is well within 0.5s. The circular silhouette is clear and distinct from both the boss entity (dark blob to the left) and the UI elements. The bright warm palette (orange-gold flames, cyan core) aligns well with the Echoes visual language of saturated, clean pixel art. No dark-area visibility concern since the scene is a mid-lit arena. The asset reads as a distinct VFX event rather than a game entity or tile, minimizing confusion. Pixel-art style is consistent with the game's native aesthetic — does not look pasted. Minor note: the outer dithered edge blends slightly with the bg at extreme scrutiny, but this is negligible in motion and at normal viewing distance.

The vfx_explosion_2_v04 sprite is a bright warm orange/gold circular burst with light blue/cyan inner sparkle highlights. The warm golden-orange and sky-blue combination aligns reasonably well with the cute fairy-tale palette (warm yellows, bright blues). The pixelated starburst shape reads as whimsical rather than grimdark. The orange flame ring is vivid and saturated — consistent with bright sunny magical tone. Minor concern: the overall scene context is a grey stone dungeon background which is slightly cold/dungeon-crawler in feel, but this is preexisting environment context, not caused by the new VFX sprite. The explosion itself adds warmth and brightness to the scene rather than shifting it darker. No horror, goth, or aggressive qualities detected in the sprite itself. The sparkle/starburst pixel art style is toy-diorama compatible. Marginal rather than full pass because the flame aesthetic edges slightly toward generic action VFX rather than distinctly fairy-tale whimsy, but does not break tone.

The explosion VFX (vfx_explosion_2_v04) is approximately 70-80px in diameter compared to the player character at ~40-50px, giving a ratio of roughly 1.5-1.6x player size. For an explosion-category effect this is appropriate — VFX are expected to exceed player footprint to convey impact, and this one stays well below screen-overwhelming scale. The circular fire-ring with bright core reads cleanly in top-down orthographic perspective, consistent with the brick-floor scene. Pixel art style matches surrounding assets. No perspective inconsistency, no scene-overwhelming bloom or radius, and the effect does not obscure critical gameplay area disproportionately. Toy-diorama feel is preserved: the effect feels like a contained in-world event rather than a full-screen wallpaper overlay.

8x zoom reveals two primary tells. (1) Inconsistent outline width: the dark border separating the orange ring from the black background alternates between 1px and 2px with no clear artistic logic — top-left edge is 1px, bottom-right transitions show a 2px dark fringe. Human pixel artists plan border weight deliberately; the irregularity pattern matches AI weight randomness. (2) Asymmetric particle scatter: the spark/debris pixels ringing the explosion are notably denser in the bottom-right quadrant with gaps top-left that aren't mirrored. A human VFX artist making a static frame would either use rotational symmetry or a clearly intentional asymmetric composition; this distribution looks stochastic. On the positive side: no sub-pixel or AA artifacts (clean integer pixel grid), no noise in solid dark background, palette is disciplined (3-4 hard tones per zone with no gradient bleed), the checkerboard shockwave interior is a legitimate pixel-art technique, and the central star silhouette reads semantically correct. The two structural tells are real but not catastrophic — score sits at the marginal/fail boundary.

Peer sheet is classic Denzi RPG pixel art: tight ~16px sprites with consistent black outlines, modest highlights, varied-but-grounded palette spanning greens/reds/browns/blues, high detail density relative to sprite size, and signature Denzi charm (distinct character silhouettes, expressive poses). Candidate vfx_explosion_2_v04 fails on 7 of 11 dims. (1) Outline weight/color: candidate has ZERO outline—edges dissolve into background pixels; every peer sprite carries a dark perimeter outline. (2) Rendering style: candidate uses oversized ~6-8px pixel blocks producing a chunky mobile-VFX look that clashes with peers' tight classical pixel art. (3) Palette overlap: candidate is almost entirely orange/gold/warm-yellow + light-blue glow—colors that appear nowhere in the peer sheet. (4) Color temperature: extreme warm bias vs. peers' neutral-to-cool mixed palette. (5) Highlight intensity: candidate is essentially a single overblown highlight mass; peers show restrained 1-2 highlight dots. (6) Detail density: given its large canvas the candidate is shockingly sparse—few distinct pixel clusters—opposite to peers' dense sprite work. (7) Charm level: it reads as a generic explosion burst with no character or wit; Denzi peers all have personality/silhouette identity. Passes only on shadow direction (both negligible) and shadow count (both ~0). Proportion difference noted but not penalized as per instructions. This asset would be a visual intruder in a Denzi-style scene.

Candidate (right panel) is a circular burst effect with a warm golden star-core and cool blue/cyan particle scatter, rendered in chunky pixel art on a parchment background. The warm gold dominates and reads immediately as treasure-star magic, squarely in Hyrule/fairy-tale register. Blue accent particles create the classic warm-cool complementary sparkle used extensively in Nintendo VFX (think Zelda rupee pop, Kirby star burst) — they do not pull the read toward cold-corporate or desaturated dungeon. Pixel fidelity and palette are consistent with the peer sprite sheet on the left: saturated primaries, clean black outlines, no gradients or photorealistic shading. No grimdark, no edge, no occult symbolism, no uncanny distortion detected. Slight risk: if the blue particle ratio were higher the effect could drift cold, but at current balance it remains warmly magical and toy-diorama-appropriate.

Emotional (60%): Bright saturated cyan-blue reads as a classic Zelda treasure gem — cheerful, warm, toy-diorama friendly. Rounded oval silhouette is approachable and charming, not threatening. Saturation is strong and fits the Hyrule palette brief. Score ~8/10 emotionally. Technical (40%): 1px hard black outline is consistent around the full perimeter with no visible anti-aliasing. Palette is tightly constrained to 3 tones: sky-blue base, darker blue shadow band at bottom, and crisp white highlights at upper-center. All pixels snap cleanly to the grid at every zoom level. Shading is hard/flat with no gradients. Matches Denzi-style hard-outline, limited-palette execution seen in reference sheet. Score ~8/10 technically. Weighted total ~8. No blocking issues found.

The sapphire gem has a genuinely charming rounded-oval silhouette — soft, friendly, not aggressive. Blue pops clearly on the dark background and holds adequately on the light cream background. The highlight rendering gives it a satisfying toy-gem personality and it passes the sticker test conceptually. However, the internal facet/cross-hatch pixel pattern introduces visual noise that muddies the read as the gem shrinks — at 1x on grey the shape is still recognizable as a gem blob but the interior charm is fully lost. The dithered outer edge on the dark panel softens the contour slightly, keeping silhouette clarity just below a clean pass. Charming shape and good color choice, but needs cleaner interior detail hierarchy (dominant highlight, simpler secondary facets) to push to pass tier.

Gem sapphire uses a well-saturated cerulean/sky-blue as its dominant hue, which maps directly to the 'sky blues' listed in the Echoes-tuned palette. Highlights are clean light-blue/white — appropriate gem sparkle, not blown-out neon. Shadow tones on the gem itself read as dark teal/deep blue rather than pure black or neutral grey, satisfying the colored-shadows requirement. The asset is bright and vibrant without muddiness or desaturation. Not cold-corporate: the blue is warm-sky rather than icy. Mood reads BRIGHT and gem-like. Palette compatibility is high — sky blue is an explicit target hue. Minor note: the gem is cool-dominant rather than warm-dominant overall, but a sapphire is intrinsically a cool-blue jewel and the brief explicitly includes sky blues; no deduction warranted.

The gem_sapphire_v01 pixel-art gem (light blue/cyan crystalline ~60px icon) sits against the dark navy hub background and is clearly readable — good luminance contrast, distinctive faceted silhouette, and blue tones pop from the dark slate tiles. Pixel-art style matches the game's golden banner elements and overall aesthetic. However: (1) the gem floats without a drop shadow or floor contact indicator, giving a slightly composited/pasted-on appearance; (2) the bottom-left joystick widget shares the same light-cyan palette, creating mild hue confusion at a glance; (3) the gem is relatively small and positioned in empty midground space — may not hit the 0.5s spottability bar on a crowded real-play screen without a subtle glow or shadow anchor. Overall readability is solid on a clean hub scene but integration polish is needed for a confident pass.

The gem_sapphire_v01 sprite is a small pixelated blue crystalline gem with faceted highlights and a light icy-blue sparkle. The color (bright sapphire blue) fits the target palette (blues/magical) and the sparkle facets align with 'whimsical sparkle.' It does not trigger any out-of-tone flags (no grimdark, no edgy, no horror). However, the sprite reads as a fairly generic pixel-art collectible gem rather than distinctly fairy-tale or toy-diorama. The surrounding scene UI (dark blue-grey panels, red Boss-lock button, dungeon-hub layout) already leans dungeon-crawler, and the gem blends into that context without actively countering it. The sprite itself is inoffensive and appropriately colorful, but lacks the warmth, roundness, or whimsical personality that would strongly anchor it in the cute sunny fairy-tale register. A more rounded silhouette, warmer rim-light, or small sparkle particles would push it to a clear pass.

issues: gem displays at ~60-65px apparent size in composite, exceeding 50-75% of 32px player rule (target 16-24px)
shown in UI narrative panel rather than true world-space placement — proportion vs in-world entities cannot be fully validated
sprite feels like a menu/HUD icon at this scale rather than a droppable world item
verdicts.reasoning: The sapphire gem sprite is composited into a dungeon-hub narrative panel illustrating the 'collect 3 relics' objective. Visually the gem occupies roughly 60-65px in the screenshot, which is significantly larger than the 16-24px envelope allowed for treasure-category items (50-75% of 32px player). In isolation the pixel-art style, color, and facing angle are solid and perspective-consistent with a top-down diorama. However the composite does not place the gem alongside a player or world tile for direct proportion comparison — it sits in a semi-UI showcase context where larger display is typical for tutorial clarity. That ambiguity prevents a hard fail, but the size would be non-compliant if reused at this scale in world-space. Toy-diorama feel is partially maintained; no wallpaper/oversaturation issues. Recommend providing a world-space composite with a 32px player reference to confirm in-game sizing before promotion.

At 8x zoom the gem body and outline are crisp pixel-aligned blocks with no sub-pixel bleed, no AA edges, and a clean dark background — no stray noise pixels detected. Hard-tone shading (dark navy outline, 3 distinct blue tones, white specular highlights) is correct for pixel art; no gradient wash found. Semantic read is solid: clearly a faceted sapphire gem with UI stack counter overlay. Two concerns keep this out of 'pass': (1) The white specular highlight cluster in the upper gem body is visibly asymmetric — left-side highlight pixel count and placement do not mirror the right side, a common AI pixel-art failure mode where the model approximates reflective symmetry rather than enforcing it. (2) A 1–2 pixel outline inconsistency appears at the lower-right facet edge where the dark outline thins to a single pixel while adjacent edges carry a 2-pixel wide dark border. Neither defect is catastrophic but together they suggest the sprite was not hand-corrected after generation.

Peer set (helmets) uses muted desaturated metallic palette: grays, silvers, dark metals, occasional warm gold and cool teal — all low-to-mid saturation. Candidate is vivid cyan-blue at full saturation, a dramatic outlier. (1) OUTLINE WEIGHT: peers use clean 1px dark outlines; candidate outline visible but harder to read against bright blue fill — marginal. (2) OUTLINE COLOR: peers use near-black; candidate appears dark blue outline, slight deviation. (3) SHADOW DIRECTION: peers imply top-left light; candidate top-lit highlight plausible, consistent. (4) SHADOW COUNT: peers show 1-2 shadow layers; candidate appears to have 1 — acceptable. (5) HIGHLIGHT INTENSITY: FAIL — peers have subtle to moderate specular; candidate has very prominent white circular/grid highlight pattern dominating the face, intensity far exceeds peer norm. (6) COLOR TEMPERATURE: peers are cool-neutral metallic; candidate is intense cool blue, beyond the cool range peers establish — pushed to extreme. (7) DETAIL DENSITY: candidate shows a grid/mosaic internal texture not seen in peers; peers use clean smooth fills with minimal internal linework — inconsistent rendering approach. (8) PROPORTION: candidate sprite is rendered visibly larger than all peers in the comparison sheet even at ostensibly matching scale — notable size outlier. (9) RENDERING STYLE: peers are clean pixel art geometric armor shapes; candidate has a chibi-face quality (large white circular highlights read as 'eyes') giving a character-like rather than item-like appearance, inconsistent with peer register. (10) PALETTE OVERLAP: near zero — no grays, silvers, dark metals, or earthy tones shared with peers; the entire hue space is different. (11) CHARM LEVEL: peers have utilitarian pixel-art charm without explicit face/character features; candidate reads as a cute face-character which, while charming, is a different register from peer helmets. Overall: 5 of 11 dimensions fail or are marginal; color and scale issues are severe enough to reject.

Candidate (right, 4x): a pixel-art sapphire gem in vivid cool blue with white highlight facets. It is not grimdark, not edgy, not corporate — it reads as a clean, competent pixel RPG collectible. However, the color temperature is notably cold/icy rather than the warm-sunny palette the target aesthetic demands. The highlight style is generic RPG inventory art rather than toy-diorama whimsy. Peer references (left) are a grid of dark metallic helmets — mostly gray dungeon-crawler armor — which themselves skew away from the bright/friendly Hyrule target, making side-by-side tonal comparison difficult; the helmets arguably pull the composition colder. The gem alone would not fail a Nintendo tone test outright, but it does not radiate the 'warm fairy-tale sparkle' of Echoes or Link's Awakening gem icons. No occult, fan-service, or GRIMDARK flags. The marginal score reflects a neutral-to-cool drift rather than an active tone violation.

Technical axis holds up well: 1px hard outlines are present and consistent across all zoom panels, shading uses 2-3 hard tones (no AA visible), and all pixels are grid-snapped — that earns a solid 7/10 on the 40% technical weight. Emotional axis is weaker (score ~5.5/10 on 60% weight): the palette is dominated by muted browns/oranges and a mid-tone green with a white chest panel. While brown + green is nature-appropriate for Hyrule, the overall impression skews earthy and rugged rather than bright/warm/charming. The reference Denzi armor pieces show snappier contrast and cleaner read-at-a-glance silhouettes. Compared to the Zelda: Echoes of Wisdom toy-diorama aesthetic — which calls for saturated yellows, pinks, vibrant blues, and friendly rounded forms — this design lacks those accent saturations and feels more generic fantasy-RPG than Hyrule-charming. The silhouette at 1× zoom is somewhat ambiguous. Combined weighted score: 0.4×7 + 0.6×5.5 ≈ 6.1, rounded to 6.

Silhouette reads adequately as chest armor — green vertical panels plus extended shoulder pauldrons give a recognizable outline. At 1x the shape is compact and identifiable as armor, which is the minimum. On dark bg contrast is solid (green + white vs dark). On light bg the white chest highlight nearly dissolves into the cream field, weakening definition there. The nature-themed leafy/feathery shoulders add some organic character but the overall impression is functional equipment, not a charming toy-like object. Shapes are angular and structural rather than round/soft. No playful personality hook that would pass a sticker test — it reads as 'generic leather armor' rather than something with a memorable silhouette or charm signature. Charming-but-mushy is not quite the failure mode; clear-but-charmless is closer. Marginal.

The armor uses a bright saturated mid-green chest plate paired with warm rust-orange leather drapes — a near-complementary split that reads as vibrant and warm, aligning well with the Echoes sunset-orange and saturated-green targets. The parchment-white chest accent is broadly compatible with palette neutrals. Color temperature overall is warm-dominant; no muddy desaturation, no cold corporate greys in the mid-tones. Main concerns: (1) pixel-art outline strokes appear to be very dark brown or pure black — the rightmost panel on the light background exposes near-black pixels that border the shadow-quality rule; if they are #0e0e0e or similar rather than a warm dark-brown/plum, that is a minor violation. (2) The white highlight on the chest is slightly neutral-cool rather than a warm cream, creating a subtle cold spot. Neither issue significantly damages the overall warm-bright read, but together they prevent a clean pass. Palette compatibility is solid: green and orange both map to reference swatches in rows 1-2; no neon or goth tones detected.

The armor_leather_v02 sprite is clearly locatable — its warm white/cream/orange/green palette pops strongly against both the dark-red dungeon tiles and the teal-gray stone floor. Silhouette is well-defined and 0.5s spottability is met. Dark-area visibility is solid; the light-colored torso reads cleanly against all background regions in frame. However, the sprite's proportions and pose closely resemble a standing character (player or NPC) rather than a distinct equipment item, which risks confusion with interactive entities in the scene. The pixel art style is close enough to the game's aesthetic but the color saturation is slightly elevated compared to surrounding tiles, giving a mild 'dropped-in' feel rather than fully native integration. No severe tonal clash, no outline loss, and the Echoes cleanliness standard is mostly maintained — this is a borderline pass/marginal case.

The armor_leather_v02 sprite uses cream/white torso, warm orange-brown shoulder pieces, and green accents — a warm, nature-friendly palette that avoids all flagged out-of-tone categories (no grimdark, no edgy aggression, no cold/corporate). The compact pixel silhouette is cute and approachable. However, leather armor is inherently earthy and muted; the saturation level falls short of the target 'bright sunny magical toy diorama' aesthetic. The sprite is tonally compatible but does not actively reinforce the warm sparkly fairy-tale mood — it sits in neutral adventurer territory. No hard pass, but no hard fail either.

The leather armor is composited as a worn skin on the player character. The character with armor occupies roughly 1.5x tile height, which is slightly tall for a 32x32 reference but within acceptable range for top-down 2D with perspective treatment. Armor proportions on the body look correct — chest padding and draping are visible without overwhelming the silhouette. The asset does not violate the 'item must not exceed player size' rule since it is a wearable. Scene retains toy diorama feel; no zoomed-in wallpaper effect. Minor concern: vertical elongation edges toward inconsistent perspective for strict top-down, but not a hard fail.

4x/8x zoom inspection of the leather armor sprite. PASS criteria met: no sub-pixel blending, no AA edges, solid green body areas are clean, no color bleed between green and brown, semantic coherence is strong (readable chest armor with shoulder pauldrons and heart motif). MARGINAL issues: (1) Shoulder fur texture on left vs right pauldron shows minor pixel-level asymmetry — a handful of brown/orange pixels differ in placement between sides, suggesting generation rather than hand-mirroring. (2) Outline consistency around the fur boundary fluctuates between 0px and 1px gap in several spots on the upper shoulder fringe, where a clean hand-crafted sprite would maintain a uniform 1px dark outline. (3) The brown/orange fur pixel cluster has a slightly stochastic scatter pattern that feels more noise-sampled than deliberately placed — individual pixels at the fur tips appear random rather than following intentional dithering logic. Core form, palette discipline (3 discrete tones per region), and structural silhouette are solid. Not a clear fail, but the fur-boundary tells push this below a clean pass.

Peer set (LEFT) consists of standalone equipment icons — helmets and shields — rendered in cool metallic grays, blues, and golds with bright specular highlights on curved surfaces. Candidate (RIGHT) shows leather armor WORN on a character torso, immediately breaking the standalone-icon presentation convention. Outline weight (1px) and outline color (near-black) are consistent with peers. Shadow direction (top-left light source) also matches. However: (1) Color temperature is warm brown/orange on pauldrons + green-teal on chest vs the cool-dominant metallic palette of peers — notable divergence. (2) Highlight style is matte/fabric rather than the metallic specular blobs seen on peer helmets/shields — expected for leather vs metal, but still reduces family cohesion. (3) Palette overlap is shallow: shared only in outline and shadow; no primary-color overlap. (4) The full-figure armor presentation vs isolated equipment icon breaks silhouette consistency. (5) Charm level: peers read as crisp, iconic items; candidate blends item + character form, diluting the iconic quality. Rendering technique (pixel art, anti-alias level, detail density) is consistent. Score 6 — passes technical rendering bar but presentation and palette choices create visible seam vs the peer family.

Candidate (right, 4x) shows a pixel-art torso armor in warm orange-brown leather with a green tunic underneath — palette and silhouette read clearly as Hyrule-adjacent Nintendo-family-friendly. Clean, bright hues; no desaturation, no grim edge-highlights, no realistic material rendering, no fan-service silhouette. The peer helmets on the left lean slightly more metallic/neutral-cool, making the candidate comparatively warmer and more on-tone than its peers rather than less. Only minor flag: one peer helmet has Viking horns and darker value which edges toward adventure-epic, but the candidate itself does not share that drift. Overall tone alignment is strong.

EMOTIONAL (60%): The sprite is a rotund, friendly-silhouetted creature with warm orange-amber tones and bright green eyes — not grim or scary, and has a toy-diorama plumpness that fits the Zelda enemy archetype. However, the palette is heavily dominated by orange/brown with only a small green-eye accent. The bright/charming Hyrule mood calls for saturated greens, blues, yellows, and pinks that are largely absent here. The overall friendliness reads as 'neutral creature' rather than 'charming Hyrule inhabitant'. Emotional score ~6.5. TECHNICAL (40%): Strong execution — crisp 1px hard outlines visible on all edges, clear 2-3 tone hard shading on the orange body and white belly with no gradient blending, pixel-grid-snapped throughout, no anti-aliasing detected. Compares favorably to the Denzi reference in terms of outline discipline and shading approach. Technical score ~8. WEIGHTED: 0.6×6.5 + 0.4×8.0 = 3.9 + 3.2 = 7.1 → 7. Marginal pass — technically solid but needs broader, brighter Hyrule color saturation to fully land the emotional axis.

Charming chubby creature with clear round-soft toy aesthetic — orange-brown body, cream belly, green eyes, stubby limbs. Personality reads instantly at 4x: sticker-test friendly, non-aggressive, distinctly cute-enemy archetype. Silhouette at 1x is readable as a stocky round biped but slightly mushy due to light belly color merging into the grey bg at small size. On dark bg: excellent contrast and pop, outline crisp. On light bg: orange body holds well but cream belly loses edge definition against the near-white bg, reducing silhouette sharpness. Overall charm is high and the design passes the friendly-toy standard, but light-bg contrast and 1x silhouette need minor edge work to fully pass.

Dominant warm burnt-orange body occupies the mid-value range exactly as required. Cream/parchment belly is warm, not cool. Shadow tones read as deep warm brown rather than pure black or neutral grey — meeting the 'colored darks' criterion. Green eye/horn accents are medium-saturation and sit comfortably in a warm-complementary relationship with the orange. All hues map clearly to entries in the reference palette-extract (warm orange, parchment cream, deep brown shadow, mid green). No cold corporate blues, no near-black goth tones, no muddy desaturated passages, no neon. The overall mood reads BRIGHT and WARM. Minor note: the green accents could be pushed slightly more vivid to reach 'candy' brightness, but at current saturation they harmonise rather than clash, so no deduction warranted.

The common_enemy_v00 sprite (labeled 黏黏球) is a warm orange/brown pixel-art character placed on the dark teal grid background. It reads clearly at a glance: the warm orange tones contrast well against the cool dark teal floor tiles, and the character has a distinct rounded silhouette with visible facial features. The red arrow marker makes the position unambiguous. Compared to the original scene (which is empty at that spot), the entity is immediately noticeable — locatability well under 0.5s. No confusion with other entities: the EchoBox to the left uses cooler muted tones, and the pressure plate UI elements are distinctly labeled. The sprite does not disappear into dark areas; it sits on a mid-value tile zone. The pixel-art style is consistent with the Echoes game aesthetic — not a pasted foreign asset. Minor note: the sprite is relatively small, but the label '黏黏球' and the label placement reinforce identification. No outline disappearance, no tonal clash, no visual clutter around the character itself.

The common_enemy_v00 sprite (黏黏球 / sticky ball) is a small, round, chunky orange-brown blob creature with stubby grey legs — the silhouette and form are cute and non-threatening, fitting the 'cute round' criterion well. It introduces no grimdark, horror, aggressive, or edgy elements. However, the color palette leans earthy/muted orange-brown rather than the bright, saturated warm tones (sunny yellows, warm pinks, vivid greens) called for by the fairy-tale toy-diorama target. The sprite is warm in hue but lacks the sparkle and vibrancy that would firmly anchor it in the bright-sunny-magical register. The surrounding environment (dark teal tiles, charcoal stone) amplifies the tone mismatch — the sprite reads more 'cute dungeon fodder' than 'whimsical fairy-tale critter'. No out-of-tone flags triggered, but the sprite needs a brighter, more saturated color treatment to fully pass mood coherence.

The common_enemy_v00 ('黏黏球' sticky-ball creature) appears to occupy roughly 1.5–2 tiles in height against the visible 32px tile grid, putting it at approximately 48–64px tall — about 150–175% of the expected 32×32 player footprint. For a common enemy category this is borderline: common enemies should feel peer-sized or only slightly larger than the player to preserve the toy-diorama sense of scale. The asset does not overwhelm the scene, perspective is consistent with the top-down view, pixel art style and chunky proportions fit the aesthetic, and no items larger than the player or summon-size violations are present. The mild oversize is the sole concern; the overall diorama feel is intact rather than 'wallpaper' zoomed-out.

Pixel art structure is generally sound — discrete pixels visible at 8x zoom, dark outline mostly consistent at 1px equivalent, background clean and low-noise. However three tells raise concern: (1) The white chest/belly region shows a radial gradient glow effect transitioning smoothly from bright center to darker edges rather than the 2-3 discrete hard-tone pixel clusters expected in clean pixel art — this is the strongest AI tell. (2) The orange body shading transitions across the torso feel slightly smooth, with more intermediate tones than a hand-authored palette would typically use. (3) The leg/appendage pairs show minor left-right asymmetry in pixel placement at 8x zoom (left rear leg cluster differs from right). Sub-pixel features absent as expected for pixel art. No severe color bleed, no melting details, no semantic failures — the spider/crab enemy reads correctly. The gradient belly shading is the key strike; everything else is borderline. Marginal rather than fail because the pixel grid is respected and no single artifact is egregious.

Peer set (LEFT) is 12 helmet/armored-head enemy sprites rendered in a cool, desaturated metallic pixel-art style: grays, silvers, gold accents, hard specular highlights, tight dark outlines, and a neutral-to-slightly-menacing charm tone. Candidate (RIGHT) breaks on nearly every stylistic axis: (1) OUTLINE WEIGHT — peers carry a consistent 1px dark outer stroke; candidate outline appears marginally thinner on organic curves, borderline acceptable. (2) OUTLINE COLOR — peers use near-black; candidate uses dark brown/black mix, minor drift. (3) SHADOW DIRECTION — candidate and peers both suggest top-left lighting, consistent. (4) SHADOW COUNT — both use single layered shadow, consistent. (5) HIGHLIGHT INTENSITY — peers show hard metallic specular dots; candidate shows soft diffuse highlights on shell and skin, significant style divergence. (6) COLOR TEMPERATURE — CRITICAL FAIL: peers are uniformly cool (steel grays, slate blues, muted golds); candidate is strongly warm (orange body, cream highlight, teal accents). No temperature overlap. (7) DETAIL DENSITY — peers have intricate engraved/riveted helmet geometry; candidate is rounded with fewer surface details, noticeably lower density. (8) PROPORTION — peers are compact head-centric silhouettes; candidate is a chibi character with visible torso/arms; acceptable as different enemy archetype. (9) RENDERING STYLE — peers are hard-surface metallic renders; candidate is soft organic creature rendering, fundamentally different material language. (10) PALETTE OVERLAP — virtually zero: peer palette is steel/silver/charcoal/crimson accent; candidate palette is orange/teal/white/warm-brown — no shared hues. (11) CHARM LEVEL — peers read as dangerous armored constructs with zero cuteness; candidate is overtly cute (large round eyes, bubbly silhouette, warm colors) — charm mismatch is severe and would visually break enemy family cohesion. Overall: 5 of 11 dimensions are failing or critically inconsistent. The candidate feels like it belongs to a different game's art style.

Candidate (right, 4x): warm orange-brown rounded pixel creature with white belly — clearly cute and non-threatening, hitting Nintendo-family-friendly and warm-Hyrule targets adequately. Color temperature is warm, silhouette is soft and rounded, no grimdark or edgy signals. However the design reads as slightly generic: a plain turtle/frog blob enemy that could appear in any mid-tier mobile platformer rather than a distinctly whimsical fairy-tale world. Lacks the toybox charm or expressive personality markers (exaggerated eyes, playful silhouette variation, storybook texturing) that separate Zelda-Echoes-tier from generic mobile-cartoon. Peer references on the left are a concern as an ambient signal: the row of gray metallic helmets skews cold and dungeon-crawly, suggesting the broader asset pool may be drifting toward desaturated-dungeon-crawler tone, which could contaminate the candidate's context even if the candidate itself is warm. Candidate alone: borderline pass; within peer context: marginal. No hard fails (no grimdark, no fan-service, no occult, no uncanny, no western-AAA grit). Nudging score to 7: passes threshold only after the peer-context penalty.

Emotional (60%): The apple reads as warm, friendly, and inviting — saturated reds and oranges with vivid green leaf accents nail the bright Hyrule mood. Round silhouette and toy-like proportions feel charming rather than realistic. No grim/dark tones; palette is cheerful and Zelda-appropriate. Score: 8/10. Technical (40%): Consistent 1px hard outlines visible at all zoom levels with no anti-aliasing bleed. Shading uses 2-3 hard tonal steps on the body (dark-red outline → mid-red → warm orange highlight) plus a small white specular dot for gloss — all pixel-grid snapped. Leaf detailing is clean with distinct pixel clusters. Compared to the Denzi armor reference, the craft level is comparable: disciplined palette, hard transitions, no sub-pixel blurring. Score: 8/10. Weighted: 0.6×8 + 0.4×8 = 8.0. Passes both emotional and technical axes.

At 1x the apple reads as a vague warm blob — the round shape suggests food but apple identity is uncertain; no distinct stem/leaf silhouette visible at that scale. At 4x the pixel art is charming: warm red body, green leaf cluster, a creamy highlight patch give it friendly toy appeal. Contrast on dark bg is good (bright red pops). On the light bg the drop-shadow helps lift it but the highlight patch blends slightly into the cream background, softening perceived contrast. Edge cleanliness is fair — the pixel outline is well-defined at 4x but the highlight mass makes the top-right silhouette feel mushy rather than crisp. Personality is present (rounded, inviting colour palette, leaf tuft) but the highlight treatment trades silhouette clarity for a shiny effect, which is the main charm-vs-clarity tension here. Passes the sticker test on dark bg, marginal on light. Rounds to marginal overall.

Apple sprite exhibits strong internal color harmony aligned with Echoes-tuned palette. Body uses warm saturated red-orange (not muddy, not neon), highlight is a peachy-salmon warm tone that reads cheerful and bright. Leaf/stem is a saturated mid-green matching the palette's greens. Shadow side of the apple uses warm dark brown-red rather than neutral grey or pure black — shadow coloring criterion met. Drop shadow on parchment background is a warm taupe, acceptable. Overall mood is unambiguously BRIGHT and WARM: classic complementary red-green pairing with warm orange accent. No cold corporate tones, no desaturated muddy areas, no goth near-blacks detected. Palette compatibility is high — all hues present in the reference swatch sheet. Minor note: drop shadow could be shifted slightly toward warm teal or plum per the colored-darks guideline, but current warm taupe is within acceptable range.

The apple sprite (~30px) is placed on the dark red dungeon tile floor where the apple body's red/pink hue partially blends with the background. The green leaf on top provides the primary differentiating detail and helps break the camouflage. Locatability without the arrow guide would likely exceed 0.5s due to color similarity with the tile background. The pixel art style is native to the game's aesthetic and does not look pasted. There is no confusion with other entities (EchoBox is a distinct rectangular shape, enemy sprites are different silhouettes). The apple's position at the tile boundary gives slight edge contrast. Overall the asset is passable but the red-on-red-background color pairing is a mild readability concern that keeps it marginal rather than a clear pass.

The food_apple_v00 sprite itself is a round, cute pixel-art apple in warm reds/pinks with a green leaf — friendly, bright, and in-tone for a cute fairy-tale aesthetic. It does not introduce grimdark, edgy, or horror elements. However, the surrounding scene is a dark dungeon-crawler environment (dark teal grid tiles, shadowy brown-red floor, low-key lighting) that sits at odds with the 'bright sunny magical toy diorama' target tone. The sprite alone is a pass-grade asset, but placed in this scene context the overall composite leans marginal. The apple does not shift mood negatively; it is actually the brightest, most cheerful element present. Flagging context mismatch rather than sprite failure.

The food_apple_v00 consumable appears roughly 38-45px in the composited scene, close to the 32x32 player reference size. Per spec, consumable items should be 50-75% of the player (16-24px). The apple reads as nearly player-sized rather than a small pickup. Pixel art style and top-down perspective are consistent with the scene. No wallpaper/zoom issues. Toy-diorama feel is preserved, but the apple's visual footprint slightly competes with entity-scale objects.

Pixel art apple sprite examined at 4x and 8x zoom. Overall structure is clean with a dark 1px outline and limited palette (dark outline, orange-red body, white highlight, green leaf). Three concerns flagged: (1) Gradient shading — the body transitions from dark orange at bottom to lighter orange-salmon toward center without strict hard-tone jumps; more intermediate color steps than a hand-crafted 2-3-tone approach would use, suggesting AI interpolation. (2) Color bleed — the white highlight cluster bleeds into the orange body with 2-3 stray off-palette pixels at the boundary rather than a clean hard edge. (3) Asymmetry — right silhouette of the apple body bulges slightly more than the left when compared pixel-column by pixel-column, consistent with AI shape generation rather than deliberate pixel pushing. No sub-pixel AA detected (pixels are full-grid), no repeating pattern artifacts, no melting or semantic failure (reads clearly as an apple). Outline thickness is consistent at 1px throughout. Score 7 reflects a usable but not pristine pixel art asset.

Peer set (Denzi helmets) exhibits hallmark characteristics: smooth anti-aliased edges, multi-layer dithered shading, crisp 1px dark outlines with consistent weight, deliberate highlight glints, moderate-to-high detail density per sprite, and a distinctive 'cute-but-functional' RPG charm. The food_apple_v00 candidate fails on multiple fronts. (1) Outline weight: peers maintain tight 1px outlines; apple outlines appear slightly blotchy/inconsistent at equivalent scale. (2) Rendering style: the apple reads as a different pixel art tradition—flatter, cruder pixel placement vs. the refined Denzi multi-tone shading approach. (3) Shadow count/quality: peers show nuanced 2–3 tone shadow gradations; the apple shadow work is rudimentary, essentially 1-tone darkening with no dithering. (4) Highlight intensity: peer helmets have distinct specular highlight pixels; apple highlight is minimal and lacks the characteristic Denzi 'pop'. (5) Detail density: peers pack rivets, trim, and decorative elements at tiny scale demonstrating high pixel-craft; apple is a largely plain shape with minimal interior detail. (6) Charm level: Denzi sprites have a characteristic chibi charm even for armor; the apple reads as a generic utility sprite without that warmth—missing the character-imbued quality of the peer set. Color temperature and palette overlap differences are excused as expected for food vs. armor item type. Proportion is acceptable. However the cumulative rendering quality gap across 5+ dimensions is too large to overlook. This sprite does not convincingly belong to the Denzi family.

Candidate apple (right, 4x) shows warm saturated red with simple rounded pixel form — no grimdark, no cold desaturation, no edgy elements. Color temperature is broadly in-target for bright/sunny Nintendo aesthetic. However the sprite reads as competent-generic pixel food rather than distinctly Zelda/fairy-tale-charming: no sparkle, no leaf warmth, no whimsical silhouette flourish that would anchor it to toy-diorama or Hyrule-warmth. Peer reference sheet (left) consists entirely of dark metallic dungeon helmets — a poor warm-tone comparator set, making cross-panel tonal grading unreliable. Apple itself does not FAIL but lacks the positive charm signal needed for a confident pass.

Emotional (60%): The gold-orange outer ring with teal swirl center is warm, saturated, and classically treasure-like — earns the bright/friendly Hyrule mood well. However the gear/cog outer silhouette skews steampunk-industrial rather than the organic, mystical relic aesthetic expected in Echoes of Wisdom. The round silhouette and warm palette are correct but the design language reads as crafted machinery rather than ancient artifact. Emotional score ~7. Technical (40%): Pixel execution is solid — 1px hard outlines visible, no anti-aliasing, 3-tone hard shading on the gold ring (bright highlight / mid gold / dark amber), 2-tone on teal center, pixel-grid-snapped throughout. Denzi peer reference confirms this category favors tight multi-tone shading with clean outlines, which the candidate meets. Technical score ~8. Weighted composite: 0.6×7 + 0.4×8 = 7.4 → 7. Verdict: marginal. Recommend revising outer silhouette toward a more emblematic/medallion form (star, triforce-adjacent motif, organic jewel setting) to lift emotional score into pass range.

At 1x the sprite is a small orange-gold blob — the gear outline is recognizable but the inner cyan/teal symbol detail is completely lost, reducing readability to shape-only. At 4x the pixel art is technically clean with a warm gold gear body and a cool cyan coin/spiral center creating good hue contrast, and the dark outline provides adequate separation on both light and dark backgrounds. However the gear silhouette is a common, slightly aggressive mechanical shape — it lacks the round/soft toylike quality that defines EoW charm. The inner spiral glyph adds interest but reads more 'mysterious artifact' than 'friendly collectible.' No strong personality cue like a face, sparkle, or bouncy proportions. Passes basic contrast and silhouette clarity tests but falls short on the charm and sticker-test criteria. Borderline marginal.

issues: shadow pixels appear near-grey/charcoal rather than colored darks (deep teal, plum, or warm brown)
verdicts.reasoning: The relic gear icon uses a strong warm-cool triad: parchment gold outer ring, sky blue/teal inner circle, and sunset orange-red trim accents — all present in the Echoes reference palette and reading BRIGHT and harmonious. Saturation is good; gold and blue are well-saturated without being neon. The overall mood is warm and inviting, consistent with the target aesthetic. However, the drop shadow beneath the gear body (visible especially on the parchment-background panel) resolves to near-charcoal grey rather than a colored dark (e.g. deep teal or warm brown). This violates the 'shadows are colored darks, NEVER pure black/grey' rule and is the sole reason for marginal over pass.

issues: circular shape has mild visual similarity to UI badge buttons at first glance
verdicts.reasoning: The pickup_relic_v00 medallion is clearly distinguishable against the dark blue-gray panel background. Its warm gold outer ring combined with a bright cyan/teal pixel-art center creates strong luminance and hue contrast against the cool dark surroundings. Locatability is high — the asset pops immediately within 0.5s. The pixel art style is consistent with surrounding game elements (待通关 badges, 回遗迹前奏 banner), lending a native rather than pasted feel. No dark-area visibility issues as the asset sits on a mid-tone panel region. The only minor issue is that the circular medallion silhouette loosely echoes the shape of the UI badge buttons, which could cause a brief ~0.3s confusion; however the distinct gold+cyan coloring and central concentric-ring motif quickly differentiate it as a pickup collectible. Overall the asset fits the bright, uncluttered Echoes aesthetic cleanly.

The pickup_relic_v00 sprite is a small pixel-art circular medallion with a gold/bronze outer ring and blue-teal inner pattern. Gold tones partially align with the target warm palette (yellows/golds are in-palette), and the circular shape nods toward 'cute round'. However, the overall design reads as a tactical RPG collectible/dungeon relic rather than a cute fairy-tale whimsical object — it lacks sparkle, softness, or warmth indicators (no pinks, no parchment, no organic curves). The base scene context is already out-of-tone (near-black background, cold dark UI, dungeon-hub aesthetic), and the new sprite does not shift mood toward the bright-sunny-magical target. The sprite is not flagrantly out-of-tone (no grimdark/horror/goth elements), but it sits in a neutral-to-slight-mismatch zone rather than reinforcing fairy-tale warmth. Score 5: marginal, does not actively harm but does not support the target aesthetic.

The composite scene shows pickup_relic_v00 (a circular gold-bronze medallion, ~60-65px diameter) rendered inside a dungeon-hub quest-objective UI panel rather than placed as an in-world floor pickup. No player character (32x32 reference) is visible in the scene. Evaluated as a UI icon the size is acceptable for readability; however the task requires evaluating it as an in-world pickup item, and at ~60-65px it would be roughly 2x the player size, violating the 50-75% rule (expected 16-24px for treasure category). The scene reads as a menu/information overlay, not a toy-diorama gameplay view, so perspective consistency and diorama feel cannot be confirmed. Bumped to marginal rather than fail because the render quality and art style of the medallion itself are appropriate, and the oversizing may be a compositing placement issue rather than an asset design flaw.

8x zoom reveals transitional/mixed pixels at the gold gear boundary against the dark background — not sharp 1-pixel steps as expected in clean pixel art, suggesting sub-pixel AA bleed likely from AI upscaling or generation. Gear teeth show slight asymmetry: top and bottom clusters are wider than left/right, and diagonal teeth vary in pixel width by 1-2 pixels. Outline thickness fluctuates between 1px and 2px at several tooth-root junctions. The blue center symbol is geometrically coherent and the 3-4 gold tone palette is acceptable. No melting, no pattern artifacts, no semantic failure. Marginal pass held back by the boundary color bleed and tooth asymmetry.

Peer set (Denzi helmets) is dominated by cool silver-gray metallic tones with dark neutral shadows and controlled specular highlights. Candidate relic is a warm amber-gold medallion with a saturated teal/blue gem center — the warmth delta is large and the teal hue has essentially zero representation in the peer palette. While a golden relic is naturally warm (item-type excuse accepted), the DEGREE of saturation on both the gold ring and the gem pushes outside the peer family's restrained color temperature. Outline weight and outline color (dark/near-black) are consistent — PASS. Shadow direction is readable top-left consistent with peers — PASS. Shadow count appears single-layer; peers use 1-2 layers — borderline PASS. Highlight intensity: the gem center's specular pop is brighter and more 'glassy' than anything in the peer set, which keeps highlights muted/metallic — mild FAIL. Color temperature: warm amber vs cool silver — marginal FAIL. Detail density: candidate has fewer distinct pixel clusters than the intricate helmet engravings — mild FAIL. Proportion: circular medallion, appropriate for relic type — PASS. Rendering style: pixel art at correct resolution with clear outlines — PASS. Palette overlap: only dark outlines and a sliver of gold trim shared; teal gem and saturated orange-gold are orphan hues — FAIL. Charm level: gem glow gives a magical toy quality slightly above the stoic-RPG tone of the peer helmets — minor mismatch. Net: style bones are right (pixel art, outlines, shadow direction) but palette temperature and gem saturation break family cohesion enough to land in marginal territory.

issues: generic-fantasy-collectible risk: golden medallion reads more 'RPG loot drop' than distinctly fairy-tale whimsical
peer helmet references on left skew dungeon-crawler/grimdark — candidate avoids this but the set context is borderline
verdicts.reasoning: Candidate (right, 4x): a warm-gold circular medallion/relic with a blue magical orb at center, pixel-art style. Positives: warm gold palette aligns with bright/sunny Hyrule target; blue gem element reads as whimsical magic; clean pixel-art execution avoids realistic or gritty textures; no dark/edgy/occult signals. Concerns: the medallion silhouette and treatment are close to generic-fantasy 'coin/medal' trope — lacks the overtly cute, toy-diorama, or fairy-tale charm specificity of a strong pass; the blue orb is appealing but slightly cold against the warm gold. Peer references (helmets, left) are notably more grimdark/dungeon-crawler than the target aesthetic, suggesting the broader asset set may have tonal drift that could eventually pull this relic into a darker context. Candidate alone is warmly neutral — on-tone but not distinctly whimsical enough to clear 8.

Emotional (60%): The bomb reads as bright, warm, and charming — saturated teal-blue round body, pink bunny-ear fuse, orange clock-face detail, warm brown base. Round silhouette gives toy-diorama quality. Palette is vibrant and Hyrule-appropriate; zero grimness. Score ~8.5/10 on emotional axis. Technical (40%): Hard 1px outlines are consistent and visible at all zoom levels. Blue body exhibits a clear 3-tone hard shading (highlight, mid, shadow) with no anti-aliasing blur. Pixels are grid-snapped; hand-pixeled craft is evident. Readability holds at small size. Compared to Denzi tech-items reference, the pixel discipline and shading structure are comparable, though the palette is intentionally much brighter to match the Zelda: Echoes of Wisdom mood. Minor note: the pink ears are thin and slightly hard to read at 1x, but do not significantly detract. Score ~7.5/10 on technical axis. Weighted: 0.6*8.5 + 0.4*7.5 = 5.1+3.0 = 8.1 → rounded to 8. Pass.

issues: 1x silhouette reads as a small blue blob — rabbit ears merge with body at tiny size
orange clock face detail lost at 1x; only color blob survives
dark bg (panel 3) reduces ear contrast — pink ears barely visible against near-black
overall shape is round/squat which aids charm but the pixel clusters produce a slightly mushy read at scale
verdicts.reasoning: At 4x on both light and dark backgrounds the icon has genuine charm: round blue bomb body, cute pink rabbit ears, friendly orange clock face, small expressive details. The Echoes-of-Wisdom toy aesthetic is clearly present and the silhouette is distinctive enough at 4x. However at 1x (panel 1) the icon shrinks to an indistinct blue-teal cluster — the rabbit ears and clock face disappear entirely, leaving only a vague colored blob with no readable personality. On the dark background the pink ears lose contrast and blend. Sticker test at 1x fails; sticker test at 4x passes. Charm is present but silhouette clarity at actual play size is insufficient for a confident pass.

The bomb icon uses a saturated sky-blue body, candy-pink rabbit ears, and a warm sunset-orange clock face — all three are direct hits on the Echoes-tuned reference palette. Outlines/shadows read as deep warm navy/brown rather than pure black, satisfying the colored-darks requirement. Warm pink and orange elements counterbalance the cool blue, keeping the overall mood bright and cheerful rather than cold or corporate. Mid-values are warm and saturated throughout. No muddy, neon, or desaturated colours detected. Minor note: blue-body dominance leans slightly cool, but the saturation level and the pink/orange accents keep it well within the BRIGHT-WARM target band.

The icon_echo_bomb_v00 sprite sits on the dark red-speckled tile floor. Its bright cyan/blue body and pink-white top create meaningful color contrast against the dark background, making it spottable in roughly 0.5s aided by the bold hue pop. The pixel-art style is consistent with the game's overall aesthetic and does not look pasted-in. However, the sprite is quite small (~32px) on a visually noisy tile pattern, which partially obscures the bomb silhouette detail — the round body and fuse become blobby. It does not significantly resemble the EchoBox or player character. The bright Echoes color palette is maintained. No severe hue clash or outline disappearance, but readability margin is thin at this scale on this tile type, warranting marginal rather than pass.

The icon_echo_bomb_v00 sprite itself is cute and whimsical — a bright cyan/blue round form with a pink fairy/floral element on top, round silhouette, saturated friendly colors. It reads as in-tone for cute fairy-tale: no grimdark, no aggression, no realism. However, the composite scene context is notably dark (dark teal grid tiles, deep crimson dungeon floor area, stone walls) which is borderline dungeon-crawler in feel, and the sprite's cheerful brightness creates a mood contrast rather than reinforcing a sunny toy-diorama environment. The sprite design itself earns an 8, but contextual placement in this dark scene pulls the overall read down to 7/marginal. The 'bomb' concept is softened by the cute fairy execution and should not be flagged on its own.

Scene shows a top-down 2D game with player character (~40px in frame, consistent with 32x32 world units) and appropriate tile grid. The icon_echo_bomb_v00 as an 'icon' category asset should be ≤24x24 per HUD rules. The composited scene places a large blue circular element in the bottom-left joystick area (~80-90px apparent size), which if representing the icon exceeds the HUD icon size constraint significantly. However this element may be the joystick control overlay rather than the icon itself, making the exact icon placement ambiguous. The overall toy diorama feel is intact — player is correctly proportioned, tile floor reads as a contained playspace rather than wallpaper, and world-space entities (EchoBox on left, pressure plate) are scaled appropriately. No perspective mismatch detected. The marginal score reflects inability to confirm the icon is rendered at ≤24x24, with the oversized circular UI element raising a concern that needs designer verification.

8x zoom shows mostly clean square pixels with hard color transitions — no gradients, no AA blur, no noise in solid fields. Palette is appropriately limited (~6 colors). However, the outline has subtle thickness inconsistencies: bottom-left arc runs 2px wide while some top edges are 1px, suggesting auto-generation rather than deliberate hand-placement. The orange ring/belt decoration has slight left-right asymmetry (right side arc is 1px shorter). No color bleed, no sub-pixel features, no melting details. Semantic read is coherent — round bomb body with fuse-nub ears and a target ring — no hallucinated limbs or semantic drift. Overall pixel fidelity is high but the outline irregularity and minor asymmetry are classic pixel-AI tells that prevent a clean pass.

Peer reference sheet shows 12 Denzi helmet sprites rendered in a consistent cool-metallic pixel art style: near-black outlines ~1px, muted grays/silver/gold/teal palette, subtle diffuse highlights from upper-left light source, single drop shadow, moderate detail density, serious/combat aesthetic. Candidate icon_echo_bomb_v00 breaks on at least 5 of 11 dims. (1) Outline weight: roughly consistent, borderline OK. (2) Outline color: dark outline present, OK. (3) Shadow direction: candidate sphere shows ambiguous rim-light treatment inconsistent with peers' directional shading — CONCERN. (4) Shadow count: single shadow OK. (5) Highlight intensity: candidate's blue sphere has a vivid specular glint and saturated face-glow far more intense than peers' subtle metallic sheen — FAIL. (6) Color temperature: peers are cool neutral metallics; candidate is a bright cerulean blue body with hot-pink and orange accent elements — very warm/saturated — FAIL. (7) Detail density: comparable, OK. (8) Proportion: different item type, not penalized. (9) Rendering style: peers read as pixel-art realism (battle-worn helmets); candidate reads as chibi/cute cartoon bomb with expressive decoration — FAIL. (10) Palette overlap: near zero — peers share no blues, pinks, or oranges; metallic grays absent from candidate — FAIL. (11) Charm level: candidate is conspicuously 'cute Echo' while every peer projects a serious armament aesthetic; charm register is misaligned — FAIL. Five hard failures across key dims. Style family coherence is broken.

Candidate (right, 4x): a round blue bomb body with a prominent pink bow/ribbon on top, warm orange circular detail, and sparkle/star accents — pixel-art rendered on parchment bg. The pink ribbon is the decisive charm anchor; it immediately reads as whimsical and toy-like rather than threatening. Colour palette is bright, warm, and saturated (sky-blue + coral-orange + magenta), consistent with sunny Hyrule / Nintendo-family aesthetic. No desaturation, no grim silhouette shaping, no realistic metallic shading. The bomb motif could in principle read edgy, but the bow completely disarms that — it maps directly onto the 'toy-diorama' register. Peer refs (left) are dark grey helmet icons that skew generic dungeon-crawler; candidate clearly outperforms peers on target tone. Minor: sparkle density is modest, could push further into 'whimsical magic' territory, but does not constitute a fail. Solidly on-tone.
